content.text: When the same prompt is sent to a large language model twice, the responses often differ — sometimes subtly, sometimes substantially. This variability is not a bug to be fixed but a fundamental property of how modern language models generate text, rooted in stochastic sampling, floating-point arithmetic on parallel hardware, server-side batching, and silent provider updates. Understanding exactly where this non-determinism originates — and how to manage it in production — is essential for anyone building reliable agentic systems.
What it is

Non-determinism in the context of LLMs means that given identical inputs (the same prompt, the same model, the same hyperparameters), the model can produce different outputs on successive calls. The difference may be as small as swapping a synonym or reordering list items, or as large as choosing a completely different approach to a task. All of these outputs may be individually valid answers — they are not errors — but their variation creates unpredictability that must be explicitly managed.

Non-determinism operates at multiple levels. At the sampling level, the model converts its internal confidence scores (logits) into a probability distribution over the vocabulary and draws from that distribution randomly. At the hardware level, GPU floating-point operations do not guarantee identical numerical results across runs because atomic accumulation order is not fixed in parallel kernels. At the infrastructure level, cloud providers silently update model weights, change routing logic, or modify system-level batching behavior without surfacing these changes through the API. Each layer adds its own source of variation.
Why it matters

For traditional software, a function with the same inputs always returns the same output — this referential transparency is the foundation of testing, debugging, and reasoning about systems. LLM-backed components break that guarantee. A CI pipeline that passes today may fail tomorrow with no code change. A user reports "it worked yesterday" and an engineer can't reproduce the failure. An agent that successfully navigated a multi-step workflow last week now chooses a different tool on step 3, breaking the state machine downstream.

In agentic pipelines, non-determinism compounds. Each LLM call introduces variability, and when the output of one call becomes the context for the next, small differences amplify. A slightly different phrasing in a planning step can cause the executor step to parse the plan differently, causing a tool call to be formatted incorrectly, which causes a retry, which changes the context window, which changes the subsequent generation. This cascade effect means that even low per-step variance can produce high end-to-end variance in complex pipelines.

The practical consequences include flaky integration tests that cannot be seeded into determinism, inconsistent tool selection in function-calling agents, user-facing quality regressions after silent model updates, and difficulty attributing production failures to specific causes. Addressing these requires deliberate architectural choices, not just retrying until a desired output appears.
Benefits

Non-determinism is not purely a liability — it is the same mechanism that makes LLMs flexible and creative. Accepting and working with variability rather than fighting it yields several genuine advantages:
Creative diversity
— multiple sampling runs over the same prompt can produce a diverse candidate pool, enabling best-of-N selection, brainstorming workflows, or ensemble reasoning.
Robustness through sampling
— when one output path fails validation, re-sampling often succeeds without any prompt modification; pure-greedy decoding can get stuck in repetitive failure loops.
Generalization
— the stochasticity that causes variability in outputs is also what prevents the model from overfitting to narrow output patterns during training, producing more generally capable models.
Graceful degradation
— systems designed to tolerate multiple valid output formats are inherently more robust to prompt drift, model updates, and edge cases than systems expecting a single exact string.
Trade-offs

The primary cost of non-determinism is unpredictability. Systems that depend on specific output formats — JSON keys in a particular order, code that compiles without modification, action names matching an enum — will experience intermittent failures that are hard to reproduce and debug. Snapshot testing of LLM outputs produces fragile test suites that break on benign model updates. Logs from production failures may never be exactly reproducible in a local environment.

Temperature controls offer a partial mitigation but not a complete solution. Setting temperature to 0 produces greedy decoding — the single most probable token is chosen at each step — but this does not eliminate all sources of variation. GPU floating-point non-determinism persists at temperature 0 because the logit values themselves can differ across runs on different hardware or with different batch compositions. Provider-side model updates affect greedy outputs too. The assumption that temperature=0 equals determinism is one of the most common misconceptions in production LLM usage.

The cost of compensating mechanisms must also be counted: structured output parsing adds latency and complexity; retry logic multiplies API costs and latency in failure cases; canary evaluation pipelines require ongoing maintenance; pinned model versions eventually become stale and must be migrated. These are real engineering costs that should be factored into system design.
How it works
Sampling mechanics
At each generation step, the model outputs a logit vector
over the vocabulary of size
. These logits are converted to probabilities via softmax with a temperature parameter
When
, the distribution becomes a point mass on the highest-logit token (greedy decoding). When

, the raw softmax probabilities are used. Values above 1 flatten the distribution, increasing randomness. Top-p (nucleus) sampling further restricts the sample to the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability exceeds a threshold
. Top-k limits sampling to the
highest-probability tokens. These truncations reduce but do not eliminate variance.
Hardware-level non-determinism

Even with temperature=0, GPU computation introduces non-determinism. Matrix multiplications on GPUs use parallel reduction operations where threads accumulate partial sums in non-deterministic order. Because floating-point addition is not associative — (a+b)+c can differ from a+(b+c) by small amounts in IEEE 754 arithmetic — different execution orderings produce slightly different logit values. This means the argmax winner can change across runs even with identical weights and inputs.

Modern serving frameworks often use custom CUDA kernels that sacrifice strict determinism for throughput. Flash attention implementations, speculative decoding, and continuous batching all have potential interaction effects that add to variance. Hardware differences (A100 vs H100 vs consumer GPUs) can compound this further because different hardware generations implement fused operations differently.
Model version drift

Cloud API providers routinely update the weights behind a named model endpoint without changing the model identifier in the API. "gpt-4-turbo" today is not necessarily the same weights as "gpt-4-turbo" three months ago. These updates can reflect RLHF re-tuning, safety fine-tuning, efficiency improvements, or bug fixes. From the caller's perspective, the inputs and API surface are identical but outputs may differ systematically — a problem that cannot be addressed by controlling temperature or seed.
Architecture
The diagram below illustrates how four distinct sources of non-determinism interact during a single LLM inference call, all leading to outputs that differ despite an identical prompt:

attrs.alt: Four sources of LLM non-determinism: sampling, GPU hardware, batching, and model drift — all producing varied outputs from an identical prompt

content.text: An identical prompt enters the LLM, which computes logits. Sampling randomness (temperature, top-p, RNG state) affects which token is drawn. GPU kernel execution order affects the logit values themselves. The batching context (which other requests share the same forward pass) can affect KV-cache state. And the model weights may have drifted since last call. The four paths converge to produce Output A and Output B — both valid, both different.
When to use it

Non-determinism is a property of LLMs, not a setting to be enabled or disabled. The question is therefore not "when to use non-determinism" but "when to lean into it vs. when to suppress it as much as possible":
Lean into variability when
Generating creative content, brainstorming, or producing candidate options for human review — diversity of outputs is the goal.
Running best-of-N selection where multiple outputs are scored and the best is kept — sampling variation increases coverage of the output space.
Building self-consistency pipelines where the same question is answered multiple times and a majority vote is taken — requires diverse samples.
Exploratory tasks where rigid determinism would cause the model to repeat a failing strategy on every retry.
Suppress variability when
Running automated tests or evals where reproducibility is important — use temperature=0, seed where supported, and deterministic output parsing.
Generating code or structured data that must conform to a strict schema — use constrained decoding or structured output APIs.
Operating in safety-critical contexts where unexpected variation could be harmful — audit outputs and validate against invariants.
Debugging production failures where reproducibility is needed to investigate root causes.
How to implement

A layered strategy addresses non-determinism at each level: control what can be controlled (sampling parameters), validate what cannot be controlled (output structure), and monitor for what changes silently (model drift). The following steps apply to any LLM-backed system:
Set temperature=0 and a fixed seed in all automated/testing contexts. Treat creative or exploratory calls separately with explicit temperature settings.
Pin model versions explicitly. Use versioned model identifiers (e.g. "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022") rather than aliased names that may silently update.
Use structured output APIs or constrained JSON generation to ensure parseable output regardless of phrasing variation.
Validate outputs against a schema before using them downstream. Reject and retry with a repair prompt if validation fails.
Write evals that test semantic intent, not string equality. Assert that a JSON key has the right value, not that the entire response matches a snapshot.
Run a small canary eval against the live model API weekly or on deploy. Alert if pass rates drop — this detects silent model updates.
Below is a Python implementation of a validated LLM call wrapper that enforces structured output and retries with a repair prompt:
Examples
Example 1 — Observing non-determinism directly
This simple script sends the same prompt ten times with temperature=1.0 and measures how often the response differs:

import anthropic
from collections import Counter

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

PROMPT = (
    "Respond with exactly one word: the name of a primary color. "
    "No punctuation, no explanation."
)

def run_n_times(n: int = 10, temperature: float = 1.0) -> None:
    results = []
    for _ in range(n):
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
            max_tokens=10,
            temperature=temperature,
            messages=[{"role": "user", "content": PROMPT}],
        )
        results.append(resp.content[0].text.strip().lower())

    counts = Counter(results)
    print(f"Temperature={temperature}, n={n}")
    for word, count in counts.most_common():
        bar = "#" * count
        print(f"  {word:<12} {bar} ({count})")
    print(f"  Unique responses: {len(counts)}")

run_n_times(n=10, temperature=1.0)
run_n_times(n=10, temperature=0.0)
# With temperature=1.0 you typically see 2-4 different colors.
# With temperature=0.0 you often (but not always) see one color dominate.
Example 2 — Self-consistency voting for more reliable answers
Self-consistency uses sampling variance to its advantage: generate multiple answers and take the majority vote. This reduces the impact of any single bad sample:

import anthropic
import re
from collections import Counter

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def extract_answer(text: str) -> str | None:
    """Extract a simple numeric or short textual answer from model output."""
    match = re.search(r"\b(\d+(?:\.\d+)?)\b", text)
    return match.group(1) if match else text.strip().split("\n")[0][:50]

def self_consistent_answer(
    question: str,
    num_samples: int = 7,
    temperature: float = 0.7,
) -> str:
    """
    Sample the LLM multiple times and return the majority-vote answer.
    More reliable than a single sample for reasoning tasks.
    """
    answers = []
    for _ in range(num_samples):
        resp = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-3-5-haiku-20241022",
            max_tokens=256,
            temperature=temperature,
            messages=[{
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"{question}\n\n"
                    "Think step by step, then state your final answer on the last line "
                    "starting with 'Answer:'"
                ),
            }],
        )
        raw = resp.content[0].text
        # Pull out the final answer line
        for line in reversed(raw.splitlines()):
            if line.lower().startswith("answer:"):
                answers.append(line.split(":", 1)[-1].strip())
                break
        else:
            answers.append(extract_answer(raw) or "unknown")

    counts = Counter(answers)
    winner, votes = counts.most_common(1)[0]
    print(f"Votes: {dict(counts)}")
    print(f"Winner: '{winner}' ({votes}/{num_samples})")
    return winner

# Multi-step reasoning benefits significantly from self-consistency
result = self_consistent_answer(
    "A train leaves at 9:00 AM traveling at 60 mph. "
    "Another train leaves the same station at 11:00 AM traveling at 90 mph. "
    "At what time does the second train catch the first?"
)
print(f"Final answer: {result}")
# Expected: ~1:00 PM (the second train needs 4 hours to cover the 2-hour head start gap)
Real-world example

Consider a customer support automation system that routes incoming tickets. An LLM classifies each ticket into one of eight categories (billing, technical, returns, account, shipping, feedback, legal, other) and extracts urgency and estimated resolution time. At launch, the system works well — the LLM consistently returns clean JSON. Three months later, the provider silently updates the model weights, and 4% of responses begin returning keys in different order, or include extra explanatory text before the JSON block, or occasionally use "Billing" instead of "billing" (case mismatch that breaks enum comparison). These are not errors in any meaningful sense — the model is still understanding the tickets correctly — but the parsing layer was brittle.

The fix required three changes: (1) switching to the provider's structured output API to guarantee schema conformity independent of phrasing, (2) normalizing extracted values (lowercasing category names, stripping whitespace) before downstream comparison, and (3) adding a weekly canary eval that sends 50 fixed tickets through the live API and asserts classification accuracy above 95%. The canary eval caught the next model update within 24 hours of its silent deployment. The lesson is that even well-functioning integrations need ongoing observability because the model is not a fixed component.
Common mistakes
Snapshot testing LLM outputs

Storing a reference response and asserting string equality on future runs produces tests that fail on every model update, every temperature fluctuation, and every time the model chooses a valid synonym. This makes the tests worse than useless — they generate noise that trains engineers to ignore failures.

# WRONG: brittle snapshot comparison
def test_classification_wrong():
    result = classify("My payment failed")
    assert result == "billing issue detected, routing to team"  # breaks constantly

# RIGHT: test the semantic value, not the string
def test_classification_right():
    result = classify_structured("My payment failed")  # returns dict
    assert result["category"] == "billing"
    assert 0.0 <= result["confidence"] <= 1.0
    assert result["urgency"] in ("low", "medium", "high")
Assuming temperature=0 means deterministic

As explained in the "How it works" section, GPU floating-point arithmetic is not deterministic even at temperature=0. Systems that rely on this assumption will be surprised by occasional variation. The correct mental model is: temperature=0 significantly reduces variance but does not eliminate it, and does not protect against model drift.
Treating every output variation as a bug

Trying to eliminate all variance through aggressive prompt engineering often produces fragile prompts that overfit to the current model version and break on updates. A system designed to accept any of several valid output structures is more durable than one that enforces a single exact phrasing.
Best practices
Use structured output APIs
(function calling, JSON mode, response schemas) as the first line of defense. Let the API enforce the schema rather than parsing free-form text.
Pin model versions explicitly
using dated identifiers. Document the pinned version and the date it was validated. Set calendar reminders for migration.
Write semantic assertions, not string comparisons
— check field values, types, ranges, and categories rather than full-text equality.
Implement retry with exponential backoff and repair prompts
— on validation failure, send the failed response back with a targeted correction instruction rather than starting over cold.
Separate evaluation from production calls
— run a dedicated eval harness against the live API on a schedule, separate from unit tests, to catch model drift early.
Log all LLM inputs and outputs in production
with timestamps and model version metadata. This is the only way to reconstruct what happened when a failure is reported.
Design downstream components to be idempotent
— if an LLM-driven action is retried, the result should be the same as if it ran once.
Performance & scalability
Mitigation strategies introduce latency and cost overhead that must be weighed against reliability gains:

Retry logic with validation adds latency proportional to the failure rate. If structured output APIs reduce the parse failure rate to under 1%, retries add negligible overhead on average. At a 10% failure rate with up to 3 retries, expected latency increases by roughly
where
is the average single-call latency — potentially a 30% increase in tail latency. Structured output APIs are therefore worth their adoption cost.

Self-consistency voting multiplies cost by the number of samples. For N=7 samples, the API cost is 7x and latency scales proportionally unless samples are run in parallel. Parallel sampling is supported by most providers: submit all N requests concurrently and await results together. For classification tasks, N=3 to N=5 parallel samples with a majority vote captures most of the reliability benefit at manageable cost.

Caching is a powerful tool: if the same prompt is expected to be sent repeatedly (e.g., a fixed system prompt + variable user input), prompt prefix caching (supported by Anthropic's API) amortizes the cost of the fixed portion across requests. This does not reduce variance but significantly reduces the cost of redundant retries when the system prompt is large.
Tooling & ecosystem
Structured output APIs
Most major providers now offer constrained generation that guarantees valid JSON conforming to a schema:
Evaluation frameworks
Several frameworks exist for running repeatable LLM evaluations to detect model drift:
promptfoo
provides a YAML-based eval harness that runs against live APIs and compares results against assertions.
LangSmith
and
Braintrust
offer hosted platforms for tracking eval metrics over time with version comparison.
Weights & Biases Prompts

integrates with W&B's experiment tracking to log LLM calls alongside ML training runs. For teams already using pytest, a lightweight approach is to write parameterized tests against a golden dataset and run them in CI against the live API on a separate nightly schedule.
Comparison with alternatives
Different strategies for managing non-determinism have different cost, reliability, and complexity profiles:
Strategy

Security & reliability
Non-determinism creates several failure modes that have security implications in production:
Output injection via variance exploitation

Adversarial inputs can be crafted to exploit the variance in LLM outputs. An attacker who knows that a model sometimes omits a safety disclaimer under certain phrasings can probe for prompt variations that reliably elicit the unguarded output. Defense in depth — output filtering, invariant checking, and rate limiting on unusual input patterns — is more reliable than trusting that the LLM will consistently refuse.
Tool call reliability

In agentic systems where the LLM selects and calls tools, non-determinism in tool selection is a reliability concern. An agent might sometimes correctly call "delete_file" and sometimes call "archive_file" for the same request, producing different side effects. Tool schemas should be maximally unambiguous, and agentic pipelines should log every tool call decision for audit. For high-stakes tool calls (those with irreversible effects), require structured reasoning output before execution and validate it against preconditions.
Reliability guarantees

A production system dealing with LLM non-determinism should define and measure its own reliability SLO — for example, "95% of classification calls return a valid structured response within 3 retries" or "the majority-vote answer on our eval set is correct at least 92% of the time." These metrics should be tracked continuously and trigger alerts on degradation. Without explicit measurement, silent model drift degrades reliability invisibly.
Key takeaways

Non-determinism in LLMs arises from at least four independent sources: stochastic sampling, GPU floating-point non-determinism, server-side batching, and silent model weight updates — and cannot be fully eliminated by any single control.
Temperature=0 reduces variance from sampling but does not make outputs deterministic; hardware-level and provider-level sources remain active.
Structured output APIs (tool use, JSON mode, response schemas) are the highest-leverage mitigation for parse-level variance and should be adopted before other strategies.
Design downstream systems to validate semantic intent and field values rather than comparing full-text output strings — snapshot tests on LLM outputs are an anti-pattern.
Self-consistency (sampling multiple responses and voting) uses variance as a feature for reliability improvement in reasoning tasks at the cost of N× token usage.
Canary evaluations run against the live API on a schedule are the only reliable way to detect silent model drift before it impacts production users.

Retry logic with repair prompts — feeding the failed output back with a correction instruction — is more effective than cold retries and converges faster than re-sending the original prompt from scratch.
Log all LLM calls with model version, timestamp, and raw output; this is the minimal observability needed to diagnose production failures and attribute them to specific model or input changes.
